claims.claim_text: The opening October 2021 logistics-company outage anecdote, including the 15-minute fix, 3-hour outage, named customer scale, and $2.4 million annual revenue figure.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, opening paragraph
claims.anchor_text: In October 2021, a global logistics company migrated their customer-facing portal from on-premises to Azure. They deployed the application across East US and West Europe regions for redundancy. On launch day, everything worked---until the East US deployment experienced a database connection pool exhaustion at peak hours. Instead of seamlessly routing users to the healthy West Europe deployment, all users saw errors. The problem was simple: they had configured Azure DNS with A records pointing directly to the East US public IP. There was no traffic routing layer to detect the failure and redirect traffic. Adding Azure Traffic Manager with health probes took 15 minutes to configure, but the 3-hour outage had already cost them their biggest customer---a shipping company that processed 40,000 packages per day through the portal. That single customer represented $2.4 million in annual revenue.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A multi-region application can still fail hard if DNS points directly to one regional IP and there is no traffic-routing layer to detect failures and steer new users to a healthy region.
claims.rationale: This is a precise, unattributed business incident narrative with named timing and revenue specifics.

claims.claim_text: Azure DNS hosts zones on a global anycast network of name servers and provides fast performance and high availability.
claims.span_hint: section: Azure DNS: Public Zones
claims.rationale: The Azure DNS FAQ directly states that Azure DNS uses Anycast on Azure's global DNS name server network for fast performance and high availability.

claims.claim_text: Creating an Azure DNS zone assigns four authoritative name servers in the azure-dns.com/.net/.org/.info pattern.
claims.span_hint: section: How DNS Zones Work
claims.rationale: Microsoft's delegation tutorial shows Azure DNS assigning four nameservers with exactly these suffixes.

claims.claim_text: After creating the zone, you must update your registrar's NS records to Azure's four name servers before public queries reach Azure DNS.
claims.span_hint: section: How DNS Zones Work, paragraph after CLI example
claims.rationale: Microsoft's domain delegation guide explicitly says you must update the parent NS records and use all four Azure nameservers.

claims.claim_text: Azure DNS alias records can target Azure resources directly and automatically track underlying IP changes.
claims.span_hint: section: Alias Records
claims.rationale: The Azure DNS alias-record overview says alias records reference Azure resources and update dynamically during DNS resolution.

claims.claim_text: Private DNS zone records aren't resolvable from the Internet and resolve only from virtual networks linked to the zone.
claims.span_hint: section: Azure Private DNS Zones, opening paragraph
claims.rationale: The Private DNS zone overview directly states that private-zone records are not resolvable from the Internet and work only from linked VNets.

claims.claim_text: Private DNS autoregistration creates A records for VMs in the linked VNet and removes them when the VM is deleted or stopped.
claims.span_hint: section: Azure Private DNS Zones, Auto-registration paragraph
claims.rationale: Microsoft's autoregistration doc explicitly describes automatic creation and removal of VM A records.

claims.claim_text: Private endpoint DNS integration overrides public service resolution so the service FQDN resolves to the private endpoint IP in the linked network.
claims.span_hint: section: Private DNS and Private Endpoints
claims.rationale: The private-endpoint DNS guidance says public DNS resolution is overridden with a private DNS zone so the FQDN resolves to the private endpoint IP.

claims.claim_text: Traffic to Azure Private Link services travels over the Microsoft backbone instead of the public internet.
claims.span_hint: section: Private DNS and Private Endpoints, final sentence
claims.rationale: The Azure Private Link overview directly states that traffic travels over the Microsoft backbone network and public exposure is unnecessary.

claims.claim_text: Traffic Manager is a DNS-based routing service that answers with endpoints according to a configured routing method.
claims.span_hint: section: Azure Traffic Manager: DNS-Based Global Load Balancing
claims.rationale: The Traffic Manager overview directly describes the service as DNS-based routing for public-facing endpoints.

claims.claim_text: Traffic Manager is not in the data path; clients connect directly to the chosen endpoint, so Traffic Manager is not a proxy or gateway.
claims.span_hint: section: How Traffic Manager Works, Critical insight paragraph
claims.rationale: Microsoft's how-it-works page explicitly says clients connect directly and Traffic Manager is not a proxy or gateway.

claims.claim_text: Azure Traffic Manager supports six routing methods: Priority, Weighted, Performance, Geographic, MultiValue, and Subnet.
claims.span_hint: section: Routing Methods table
claims.rationale: The Traffic Manager routing-methods documentation lists these six supported methods and their intended behaviors.

claims.claim_text: The retail-company Traffic Manager anecdote claims transparent failover, zero downtime, and 45 minutes of recovery headroom.
claims.span_hint: section: Traffic Manager with Weighted Routing, War Story paragraph
claims.anchor_text: **War Story**: A retail company used Traffic Manager with Performance routing for their global storefront. During a product launch, their East US deployment became overloaded. Traffic Manager's health probes detected the degradation and automatically started routing new DNS queries to West Europe. The failover happened transparently---customers experienced a brief increase in latency (transatlantic vs same-region) but zero downtime. The engineering team had 45 minutes of breathing room to scale up East US before most users even noticed the region switch.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Scenario**: With DNS-based regional failover, new DNS queries can be steered toward a healthier region when probes detect an endpoint problem, which can reduce downtime while the primary region recovers.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed incident-style anecdote with precise operational outcomes.

claims.claim_text: Azure Front Door provides Layer 7 web-application delivery features such as SSL offload, path-based routing, caching, and WAF integration.
claims.span_hint: section: Azure Front Door: The Modern Alternative, intro and comparison table
claims.rationale: Microsoft's load-balancing overview describes Front Door as a Layer 7 application delivery network with SSL offload, path routing, caching, and WAF-related security.

claims.claim_text: The comparison table gives exact current pricing of ~$0.36 per million Traffic Manager queries and ~$35 per month plus per-request charges for Front Door.
claims.span_hint: section: Azure Front Door comparison table, Cost row
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Cost** | Lower-cost DNS-based pricing; see current Azure pricing | Higher base fees plus request and data charges; see current Azure pricing |
claims.rationale: The teaching point is comparative cost shape, but these exact price points are time-sensitive and region-dependent.

claims.claim_text: The comparison table gives exact failover-speed numbers of 30-300 seconds for Traffic Manager and under 30 seconds for Front Door.
claims.span_hint: section: Azure Front Door comparison table, Failover speed row
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Failover speed** | Depends on DNS caching and health-check settings | Typically faster application-layer failover with active health probes |
claims.rationale: The exact timing ranges are not cleanly backed by one current primary source, but the comparative architectural lesson is valid.

claims.claim_text: Azure DNS hosts over 100 million zones as of 2024 and delivers typical query latency under 20 ms worldwide.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?, item 1
claims.anchor_text: 1. **Azure DNS hosts over 100 million DNS zones** as of 2024, making it one of the largest authoritative DNS providers in the world. Azure DNS uses anycast networking, meaning queries are automatically routed to the closest DNS server. The result is typical query latency under 20 milliseconds from anywhere on the planet.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 1. **Azure DNS is a large-scale authoritative DNS service** that uses anycast networking so queries are answered by nearby DNS servers, which improves performance and availability.
claims.rationale: The exact scale and latency figures are not supported by a current allowlisted primary source, but the anycast lesson is.

claims.claim_text: Traffic Manager health probes come from published Microsoft IP ranges and the AzureTrafficManager service tag; firewalls must allow them or probes can fail.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?, item 2
claims.rationale: The endpoint-monitoring doc directly states that firewalls must allow Traffic Manager IPs and recommends the AzureTrafficManager service tag.

claims.claim_text: Azure Front Door currently has 192 edge locations across 109 metro cities.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?, item 3, opening sentence
claims.rationale: The current Microsoft Learn edge-location page explicitly gives these counts.

claims.claim_text: The module says a Tokyo user's TLS handshake drops from about 200 ms to about 5 ms when using Front Door.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?, item 3, middle sentence
claims.anchor_text: This reduces the round-trip time for the SSL negotiation from ~200ms (to a US backend) to ~5ms (to a local PoP).
claims.suggested_rewrite: This can substantially reduce TLS setup latency for users by terminating TLS at a nearby edge location instead of at a distant origin.
claims.rationale: The exact latency numbers are illustrative and not supported by a current primary source.

claims.claim_text: A VNet can be linked to only one private DNS zone with autoregistration enabled.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?, item 4
claims.rationale: Microsoft's autoregistration documentation explicitly states this one-registration-zone-per-VNet limit.

claims.claim_text: Traffic Manager's default TTL is 300 seconds, and higher TTL values make failover slower because cached answers persist longer.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, TTL row
claims.rationale: Microsoft's performance-considerations doc states the default TTL is 300 seconds and that longer caching delays traffic redirection away from failed endpoints.

claims.claim_text: Every VNet that needs to resolve names from a private DNS zone must be linked to that zone.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, Private DNS linking row
claims.rationale: The Private DNS zone overview says resolution works only from VNets linked to the zone.

claims.claim_text: DNS standards prohibit placing a CNAME at a node that has other data, which is why a zone apex cannot be a normal CNAME.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, CNAME-at-apex row
claims.rationale: RFC 1034 states that if a CNAME is present at a node, no other data should be present.

claims.claim_text: Azure DNS alias records can be created at the zone apex to point to supported Azure resources such as Traffic Manager profiles.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, CNAME-at-apex row
claims.rationale: Microsoft's alias-record documentation explicitly describes using alias records at the zone apex.

claims.claim_text: The common-mistakes row says endpoint status defaults to Enabled and therefore Traffic Manager assumes the endpoint is healthy if probes are not configured.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, health probe row
claims.anchor_text: | Not configuring health probes on Traffic Manager endpoints | Endpoints default to "Enabled" which means Traffic Manager assumes they are healthy | Always configure health probes with a meaningful path (like /health) that checks actual application readiness, not just that the server is responding. |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Not using a meaningful Traffic Manager health check | A generic or misconfigured probe can stay green even when the application is unhealthy | Configure HTTP/HTTPS monitoring with a path like /health that validates real application readiness. |
claims.rationale: The current monitoring docs describe default health-check behavior more precisely than this row's simplified claim.

claims.claim_text: New or updated Azure DNS zones and records appear on Azure DNS nameservers within 60 seconds, but client and resolver caches can continue serving older data until TTL expiry.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, DNS propagation row
claims.rationale: The Azure DNS FAQ directly gives the 60-second Azure-side update window and notes external caching remains TTL-dependent.

further_reading.title: Azure DNS FAQ
further_reading.why_relevant: Best primary source for Azure DNS behavior, delegation, propagation timing, and alias-record FAQs.

further_reading.title: Azure Traffic Manager Overview
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers the core DNS-based routing model, public-endpoint scope, and service purpose.

further_reading.title: Azure Private Endpoint DNS Zone Values
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains how private endpoints rely on private DNS zones to override public-name resolution.

further_reading.title: Azure Front Door Overview
further_reading.why_relevant: Best official overview of Front Door capabilities, edge network, SSL offload, caching, and security positioning.

notes: All proposed URLs are allowlisted primary sources. No shared citation pool appears to exist for this module, so section_pool_ref is null. Unsourced incident anecdotes and unstable price/latency marketing numbers were rewritten or generalized instead of being force-cited.
